AGENT-AUTH.
Specification Implementations Examples Registry GitHub
v1.0-draft / Working Draft / 6 March 2026

Agent Auth Protocol

Version: 1.0 (Draft**)**

This document is an implementation-oriented working draft. It is designed to be adoptable by products and platforms today, especially services that already expose MCP tools, OpenAPI operations, or HTTP APIs. The goal is practical agent authentication and authorization.

1. Introduction

1.1 Problem Statement

AI agents are becoming long-lived actors: copilots, background workers, scheduled automations, and multi-step systems that call external services without constant human supervision. To operate safely, a service needs to recognize the agent itself as a principal, whether the agent is acting on behalf of a user (delegated) or operating on its own (autonomous).

Today, most agent integrations reuse a user's OAuth token or a shared client credential. That collapses the runtime agent into someone else's identity and creates three problems:

  1. No visibility. The server can't tell which agent made a request. There's no audit trail per agent — every call looks like it came from the same OAuth client.
  2. No scoping. Every agent gets the user's full permissions or the client's permissions. You can't give one agent read-only access and another full access.
  3. No isolation. You can't revoke one agent without revoking all of them. One compromised agent means rotating credentials for everything.

This gap is especially acute for autonomous agents. They may discover capabilities, request access, run on schedules, escalate permissions mid-task, or later connect to a user account. Existing user-and-app auth models do not cleanly represent that lifecycle, because they model the human user and the application, but not the runtime agent in between.

1.2 Solution

Agent Auth makes the runtime agent a first-class principal. Instead of borrowing a user's session or a shared application token, each agent gets its own identity, its own granted capabilities, and its own lifecycle. That gives the server something existing auth models are missing: a durable way to reason about which specific agent is acting, what it is allowed to do, and how to suspend, expire, or revoke it without affecting everything else.

The mechanism is simple: each agent has its own cryptographic identity (Ed25519 keypair), and the client also has its own registered host identity. The host creates and manages agents; the agent authenticates its requests with short-lived signed JWTs. This lets the server attribute every call to a concrete chain of trust: which host created the agent, whether it was delegated or autonomous, which capabilities were granted, and which capability was invoked.

In other words, the protocol separates identity (who is this agent?), authorization (what can it do?), and execution (how does it call the service?) so services can safely support both user-linked agents and autonomous agents with the same model.

Agent Auth is intentionally practical and product-shaped. It is closer in spirit to MCP than to OAuth core: it specifies the pieces an implementation needs to interoperate today, including capability discovery, registration, approval flows, execution handoff, and lifecycle management. It is not trying to be the smallest possible greenfield standards primitive.

The protocol defines two layers:

  • Server API — how a service registers agents, manages capabilities, and verifies identity
  • Client interface — standardized tools that agents call to discover services, register, and execute capabilities. The client has its own registered identity (host keypair) on each server.

The protocol separates identity (who is this agent?) from authorization (what can it do?) from execution (how does it call capabilities?).

1.3 Conformance

The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 RFC 2119 RFC 8174 when, and only when, they appear in capitalized form, as shown here.

1.4 Terminology

  • Principal — An identity the server can authenticate, authorize, audit, and revoke independently. In this protocol, both hosts and agents are principals.
  • Agent — The runtime AI actor that needs to call services. Each agent has its own server-side agent_id, its own keypair, its own granted capabilities, and its own lifecycle.
  • Client — The tool layer the agent calls (MCP tools, CLI, SDK, IDE integration). It manages host and agent keys, talks to servers, and signs JWTs. A client is a running process.
  • Server — The service's authorization server. It manages discovery, host and agent registrations, approvals, capability grants, and JWT verification. Also called the auth server.
  • Host — The client's stable identity on a server: a keypair plus associated metadata. A host represents a device, application, or environment that creates agents (e.g. "ChatGPT," "MacBook Pro," "Claude Code"). It is not a running process. A host MAY exist without any linked user and MAY be linked to at most one user via user_id. An unlinked host MAY provision autonomous agents. A linked host represents a specific user's device or application on that server.
  • Capability — A server-offered action or function, described by an id, title, description, and invoke descriptor.
  • Agent Capability Grant — A stored grant of one capability to one agent. Agent capability grants carry per-capability state and metadata such as status, granted_by, and expires_at. See §7.3.
  • Registration — The process by which a host creates or re-establishes an agent identity on a server. A successful registration results in an agent_id, a mode, and an initial permission set.
  • Approval — User consent for registration, trust establishment, account linking, or capability escalation. Approval is required when server policy does not allow the request to be auto-approved.
  • Mode — How an agent was registered: "delegated" (acting on behalf of a user) or "autonomous" (operating without a user in the loop). Mode is immutable after creation.
  • Linked host — A host with a user_id set. A linked host MAY auto-approve delegated agents within its default capability set. Linking happens via server-side mechanisms or delegated registration approval (§3.3).
  • Host JWT — A JWT signed with the host's private key. Used for host-authenticated requests such as registration, discovery with identity, status checks, and management operations.
  • Agent JWT — A short-lived JWT signed with the agent's private key. Used to authenticate capability execution and other agent-authenticated requests.
  • JWK / JWKS — Standard JSON formats for public keys and key sets (RFC 7517). Keys may be sent inline as JWKs or published at a JWKS URL for lookup and rotation.
  • Issuer — The base URL that identifies the server's authorization authority. All protocol endpoints are relative to the issuer, and JWT aud claims are checked against it.
  • Provider — The service being connected to, identified in discovery by provider_name. A generic client may talk to multiple providers.
  • Grant status — The per-capability authorization view returned by /agent/capabilities when called with an agent JWT, derived from whether the agent has been granted that capability.

1.5 Architecture

The protocol defines three runtime participants and two identity types:

Kind Name Role
Participant Agent The AI that needs to act. Has an agent ID and calls client tools.
Participant Client The tool layer the agent calls (MCP tools, CLI, SDK). Communicates with servers, manages keys, and signs requests.
Participant Server The service's authorization server. Manages registrations, capabilities, approvals, and verification.
Identity type Host A stable identity a client uses to create and manage agents on a server. The server associates default capabilities, trust, and optionally a user_id with it.
Identity type Agent A per-agent identity used for authenticated capability execution after registration.

The host is an identity record and credential set, not a running process. A client uses a host identity when registering agents, checking status, or performing host-authenticated operations.

In practice, a host is usually backed by a keypair whose private key is available to the client. The client uses that key to sign host-authenticated JWTs. The server stores or resolves only the corresponding public key.

On each server, the host's public key identifies the host. The server MAY associate default capabilities and a user_id with that host. When a client signs a registration request with the host's private key, the server recognizes the host and can auto-approve delegated agents within that host's default capability set when the host is already linked to a user.

Public keys for both hosts and agents can be provided to servers in two ways:

  • Inline (JWK) — sent directly during registration. Simplest case.
  • JWKS URL — published at a URL the server can fetch. The URL MAY be served by the client itself, or by a service acting on the client's behalf (for example a broker or identity service). This is useful for key rotation and for avoiding manual public-key distribution across multiple verifier services. The private keys still live with the client or the service that controls that identity.

1.6 Protocol Flow

     +-------+          +--------+            +---------------+
     |       |-(A)----->|        |-(B)------->|               |
     | Agent | connect  | Client | register   | Authorization |
     |       |          |        | (Host JWT  |    Server     |
     |       |<-(E)-----|        |  + agent   |               |
     +-------+ agent_id +--------+  pub key)  +-------+-------+
               + grants           |<-(C)------|
                                  | agent_id  |
                                  | + grants  |
                                  |           +---------------+
                                  |
                                  |  [If Pending]
                                  |
                                  |            +---------------+
                                  |   (D)      |   Resource    |
                                  +----------->|     Owner     |
                                  |<-----------| Approval      |
                                  |            |    (User)     |
                                  |            +---------------+
                                  |
     +-------+          +--------+
     |       |-(F)----->|        |
     | Agent | sign_jwt | Client |
     |       |<-(G)-----|        |
     +-------+ JWT      +--------+

     +-------+                                +---------------+
     |       |-(H)--- Signed Agent JWT ------>|   Resource    |
     | Agent |                                |    Server     |
     |       |<-(K)-- Protected Resource -----|               |
     +-------+                                +------+--------+
                                                     |
                                                (I) introspect
                                                  (agent JWT)
                                                     |
                                              +------+--------+
                                              | Authorization |
                                         (J)  |    Server     |
                                              | active/grants |
                                              +---------------+

              Figure 1: Agent Auth Abstract Protocol Flow
  • (A) Agent requests connection via client tools (connect_agent with requested capabilities, reason, and mode).
  • (B) Client registers agent with the authorization server, presenting a host JWT containing the agent's public key and requested capabilities.
  • (C) Authorization server returns agent ID and capability grants — either active (auto-approved) or pending (approval required).
  • (D) If pending, the resource owner (user) approves via device authorization (RFC 8628) or CIBA. The client facilitates the approval flow and polls for completion.
  • (E) Client returns agent ID and granted capabilities to the agent.
  • (F) Agent requests a signed JWT from the client (sign_jwt).
  • (G) Client signs a short-lived agent JWT (§4.3) with the agent's private key and returns it.
  • (H) Agent presents the signed JWT to the resource server.
  • (I) Resource server calls the authorization server's introspect endpoint (§5.10) with the agent JWT.
  • (J) Authorization server validates the JWT and returns the agent's status and granted capabilities.
  • (K) Resource server checks the introspection response and returns the protected resource.

Resource servers that are co-located with the authorization server MAY verify the JWT directly instead of calling introspect (steps I–J).

Notes:

  • Discovery is a pre-step for generic clients. Multi-provider clients MAY call list_providers (§6.1) and discover (§6.2) before step (A). Single-service clients skip discovery.
  • Agents invoke capabilities using their own tools. The protocol does not define a built-in execute tool. The agent reads the capability's invoke descriptor and uses whatever tools it has available (HTTP clients, browser tools, code execution) to make the call with the signed JWT.
  • Capability escalation. An active agent that needs additional capabilities calls request_capability (§6.6), which may trigger another approval flow (D) for the new capabilities.

Detailed use case flows with sequence diagrams are in Appendix A.

2. Agent

2.1 What is an Agent

An agent is a runtime AI actor that needs to call external services. Each agent has its own server-side agent_id, its own keypair, its own granted capabilities, and its own lifecycle. Agents are created through hosts (§3) and authenticate with short-lived signed JWTs (§4.3).

2.2 Modes

An agent operates in one of two modes, which determines its relationship to a user. The mode is chosen at registration and cannot be changed after creation.

Delegated mode ("delegated", default) — the agent acts on behalf of a specific user. The user sees what the agent is requesting and decides what to allow. This is the common case: a user's copilot, assistant, or tool-calling agent.

Autonomous mode ("autonomous") — the agent operates without a user in the loop. Capabilities are granted by server policy or administrator approval, not by an end user. Use cases include background workers, scheduled automations, service-to-service agents, and agents that bootstrap before any user interaction. If the agent is later claimed by a user, it transitions to "claimed" state (§3.4).

How each mode affects registration and host linking is covered in §3 (Hosts) and §6.3 (Registration). Servers advertise which modes they support via discovery (§5.1) and MUST reject registration requests with an unsupported mode.

2.3 States

Every agent is in exactly one state at any time. Only active agents can authenticate and make requests.

State Description
pending Awaiting user approval. Cannot authenticate.
active Operational. Each request extends the TTL.
expired Session TTL or max lifetime elapsed. Re-approval required to reactivate.
revoked Permanent. Cannot be reactivated.
rejected User denied the registration.
claimed Autonomous agent claimed when its host acquired a user_id (§3.4). Capabilities revoked, agent archived. Activity history attributed to the user. Terminal state.

2.4 Lifetime Clocks

Three independent clocks govern agent lifetimes. The durations are server policy — the server MAY vary them per agent based on mode, host, capabilities, or any other criteria. The agent only sees the computed expires_at in responses.

Clock Measured From Resets On Reactivation Effect When Elapsed
Session TTL last request Yes → expired (idle timeout)
Max lifetime last activation Yes → expired (caps active sessions)
Absolute lifetime agent creation Never → revoked (permanent death)
  • Session TTL protects against abandoned agents. If the agent stops making requests, it expires.
  • Max lifetime caps continuous use. Even if the agent is active, after this duration it must be re-authorized. This prevents compromised agents from running indefinitely.
  • Absolute lifetime is a hard cap from creation. Once elapsed, the agent is permanently revoked — it cannot be reactivated. A new agent must be created.

2.5 Reactivation

An expired agent can be reactivated, but reactivation is a security checkpoint. The agent returns to its baseline capabilities — any previously escalated capabilities are lost and must be re-requested. This ensures that after a period of inactivity or prolonged use, the agent does not silently retain elevated access.

The session TTL and max lifetime clocks reset on reactivation. The absolute lifetime clock does NOT reset — if the agent's absolute lifetime has elapsed, reactivation MUST fail with absolute_lifetime_exceeded.

The full reactivation mechanics — how the host determines what gets auto-approved vs. what requires user approval — are covered in §5.3.

2.6 Revocation

An agent can be revoked by:

  • Itself — the agent requests its own revocation through the client
  • Its host — the host that created the agent
  • The owning user — through the server's management UI or API
  • An admin — as defined by the server

Revocation is permanent — a revoked agent cannot be reactivated. The specific endpoints and mechanisms are defined in §5.6 and §6.7. Host revocation and its effect on agents is covered in §3.

3. Host

3.1 What is a Host

A host is a registered keypair that represents a device, application, or environment (e.g. "ChatGPT," "MacBook Pro," "Claude Code"). Every agent is created through a host. A host MAY exist without any linked user (unlinked host) or be linked to at most one user (linked host). Hosts carry default capabilities — agents created through them can be granted these capabilities without additional approval.

3.2 Host Establishment

Hosts are established in one of two ways:

  1. Dynamic registration — the host is first seen as part of agent registration (§5.3). If the host is unknown, the server creates it in pending state and waits for user approval.
  2. Pre-registration — the host is registered before any agent exists. The user provides the host's public key directly via the server's dashboard, admin API, or any other server-specific mechanism.

In both cases, the result is the same host record: a host keypair (or JWKS URL), an optional linked user via user_id, and a set of default capabilities for delegated agents. A host without a linked user MAY provision autonomous agents.

3.3 Host Linking

Linking binds an unlinked host to a specific user. It happens through one of two mechanisms:

  1. Server-side linking — the server links the host through its own dashboard, admin API, or any implementation-specific mechanism (e.g. a desktop app, browser extension, or local daemon running on the device).
  2. Delegated registration approval — an unknown host registers a delegated agent (§5.3). The user approves the registration, and the server links the host to the approving user.

Once linked, future delegated agents through this host can be auto-approved for the host's default capabilities.

A host MAY be linked to at most one user. A user MAY have multiple hosts (e.g. different devices or clients).

3.4 Autonomous Agent Claiming

When a host becomes linked, all active autonomous agents under that host MUST be claimed:

  1. Each autonomous agent's capabilities are revoked
  2. Each autonomous agent's status is set to "claimed"
  3. Each autonomous agent's activity history is attributed to the user
  4. Any resources created or owned by the autonomous agent SHOULD be transferred to the user

This is analogous to anonymous-to-authenticated user linking: everything the agent did before a user was in the picture becomes the user's. Servers MAY use a system account or anonymous placeholder to hold autonomous agent ownership until a real user claims it.

Claiming is automatic — it is a direct consequence of host linking, not a separate decision. Once claimed, the autonomous agent is terminal. If the user wants to continue using the service, the host registers a new delegated agent.

3.5 Host States

Every host is in exactly one state at any time. Only active hosts can create agents. When a host is revoked, all agents under it are also revoked.

State Meaning
active Operational. Can create agents.
pending Awaiting approval.
revoked Permanently disabled.
rejected Approval was denied.

4. Authentication

4.1 Keypairs

Both hosts and agents use Ed25519 keypairs in JWK format.

  • Algorithm: EdDSA with curve Ed25519
  • The private key MUST never be sent to the server
  • The server stores only public keys (or JWKS URLs)
  • The agent's private key lives with the host/client

The algorithms field in discovery (§5.1) lists what the server accepts for new registrations. Removing an algorithm from the list MUST NOT invalidate existing agents or hosts that registered with it.

4.2 Host JWT

Hosts authenticate by signing short-lived JWTs per RFC 7519 with the host's private key. The JWT header MUST include kid when using a JWKS URL.

Required standard claims: aud (server's issuer URL from discovery), iat, exp, jti.

Protocol-specific claims:

Claim Type Required Description
host_public_key JWK Conditional Inline host public key. Required unless host_jwks_url is provided.
host_jwks_url string Conditional JWKS URL for the host's public keys. Required unless host_public_key is provided. Server matches by kid from the JWT header.
agent_public_key JWK Conditional Inline agent public key. Required for registration (§5.3) unless agent_jwks_url is provided. Not required on other endpoints.
agent_jwks_url string Conditional JWKS URL for the agent's public keys. Required for registration (§5.3) unless agent_public_key is provided. Not required on other endpoints.
agent_kid string Conditional Key ID for the agent's key in the agent JWKS. Required when agent_jwks_url is provided.

Host and agent JWKS URLs MUST point to different endpoints. Servers that fetch JWKS URLs MUST apply URL fetch protections (§9.12) and SHOULD cache previously fetched keys. Servers MUST accept host JWTs with or without agent key claims on non-registration endpoints.

4.3 Agent JWT

Agents authenticate by signing short-lived JWTs per RFC 7519. JWTs SHOULD expire within 60 seconds. There is no refresh mechanism — the client signs a new JWT per request.

Required standard claims: sub (agent ID), aud (server's issuer URL from discovery), iat, exp, jti.

Protocol-specific claims:

Claim Required Description
capability_ids No String array of capability IDs this JWT is authorized for. If present, the server MUST reject requests for capabilities not in this list — even if the agent has them granted. If absent, the JWT is valid for all the agent's granted capabilities.
cnf No Optional confirmation claim for proof-of-possession profiles. When present, it binds the JWT to a proof key or client certificate, following RFC 7800.

4.4 Proof of Possession (Optional)

For higher-security deployments, servers MAY require a standard proof-of-possession profile:

  • DPoP (RFC 9449) — the client sends the agent JWT as the access token and a separate DPoP proof JWT in the DPoP header on each request. The agent JWT SHOULD include cnf.jkt (the JWK thumbprint of the DPoP proof key).
  • mTLS (RFC 8705) — the client authenticates with a TLS client certificate. The agent JWT SHOULD include cnf.x5t#S256 to bind it to that certificate.

Servers that require proof of possession SHOULD use one of these standard profiles rather than placing htm, htu, or ath directly inside the agent JWT.

4.5 Verification

  1. Extract sub from JWT (agent ID)
  2. Verify aud matches the server's own issuer URL — reject if it doesn't
  3. Look up agent
  4. Check status (revoked → reject, expired → reject, pending → reject)
  5. Verify JWT signature against stored public key (or JWKS)
  6. Check exp, iat, jti replay
  7. If proof of possession is required by server policy, validate the DPoP proof or mTLS binding and ensure it matches the JWT's cnf claim
  8. Resolve agent's granted capabilities
  9. If capability_ids claim present in JWT, intersect with granted capabilities — reject if the requested operation is not in the JWT's capability_ids list
  10. Process the request

The server MUST ensure that revocation takes effect within a bounded window.

4.6 Replay Detection

All JWTs MUST include jti. The server MUST cache seen values and reject duplicates within the JWT's max age window.

5. Server

Clients obtain the server configuration from discovery (§5.1). That configuration includes the server's issuer URL. All endpoint paths in this section are relative to that issuer.

The Server API is organized into four groups:

  • Discovery (§5.1) — how clients find the server's configuration
  • Capability and registration flow (§5.2–§5.4) — capabilities, registration, and capability requests
  • Lifecycle operations (§5.5–§5.9) — status, revocation, and key rotation
  • Introspection (§5.10) — token introspection for resource servers
  • Error format (§5.11) — standard error responses

5.1 Discovery

GET /.well-known/agent-configuration

Servers SHOULD publish this endpoint. No authentication required. Returns the service's Agent Auth configuration.

This endpoint enables generic clients to work with any Agent Auth server. Purpose-built clients (e.g. an MCP server built specifically for one service) MAY skip discovery and use pre-configured endpoints instead.

Response:

{
  "version": "1.0-draft",
  "provider_name": "bank",
  "description": "Banking services — accounts, transfers, and payments",
  "issuer": "https://auth.bank.com",
  "algorithms": ["Ed25519"],
  "modes": ["delegated", "autonomous"],
  "approval_methods": ["device_authorization", "ciba"],
  "endpoints": {
    "register": "/agent/register",
    "capabilities": "/agent/capabilities",
    "request_capability": "/agent/request-capability",
    "status": "/agent/status",
    "revoke": "/agent/revoke",
    "revoke_host": "/agent/host/revoke",
    "rotate_key": "/agent/rotate-key",
    "rotate_host_key": "/agent/host/rotate-key",
    "introspect": "/agent/introspect",
    "device_authorization": "/device/code",
    "ciba_authorize": "/agent/ciba/authorize"
  },
  "jwks_uri": "https://auth.bank.com/.well-known/jwks.json"
}
Field Type Required Description
version string Yes Version of this protocol draft implemented by the server (e.g. "1.0-draft"). See §5.1.1 for versioning.
provider_name string Yes Unique provider identifier.
description string Yes Human-readable description of the service. Shown in list_providers results.
issuer string Yes Base URL of the authorization server
algorithms string[] Yes Supported key algorithms
modes string[] Yes Supported registration modes: "delegated", "autonomous", or both
approval_methods string[] Yes How user approval works. Defined values: "device_authorization" (RFC 8628), "ciba" (Client Initiated Backchannel Authentication). Servers MAY include additional custom methods (see §8.4). Clients SHOULD ignore methods they don't recognize.
endpoints object Yes Server API endpoint paths, relative to issuer
capabilities object[]? No Optional lightweight capability summaries (for example id, title, description). Full invoke details remain on /agent/capabilities.
jwks_uri string No URL to the server's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set). Enables clients to verify server-signed responses in future protocol extensions. Servers that plan to sign responses SHOULD include this.

Invoke endpoints (MCP endpoints, OpenAPI URLs, HTTP URLs) are not part of the discovery document. They are described per-capability in the /agent/capabilities response (§5.2). The discovery document is strictly for identity and authorization configuration.

5.1.1 Versioning

The version field identifies the draft version of the protocol, using the format MAJOR.MINOR with an optional -draft suffix (for example "1.0-draft").

  • Clients MUST check version before proceeding.
  • If the major version is unsupported, the client MUST stop and report the incompatibility to the agent.
  • Draft versions MAY introduce breaking changes between releases.
  • Clients SHOULD ignore unrecognized fields in responses where possible.

5.2 Capabilities

GET /agent/capabilities

Returns capabilities the server offers. Each capability has a small protocol-level core (id, title, description, invoke). When the request is authenticated with an agent JWT, each returned capability also includes its per-agent grant_status.

Terminology: A capability is what the server offers (id, title, description, invoke). Capability IDs are the protocol-level grant unit: registration requests, grant records, and JWT restriction all use capability IDs.

The protocol defines the core capability schema and the invoke descriptor. Agent Auth does not require a single execution mechanism for all capabilities. The invoke object tells the agent what kind of action the capability represents and provides enough information for the agent to figure out how to invoke it using whatever tools it has available.

To improve baseline interoperability, this spec defines a recommended standard invoke profile for invoke.type = "http" (§5.2.2). Generic clients SHOULD support that profile out of the box. Servers MAY also publish other invoke types for richer or proprietary integrations.

Supports three authentication modes:

Auth What's returned
No auth Public capabilities only (if the server exposes any)
Host JWT (Authorization: Bearer <host_jwt>) Capabilities available to the host's linked user. Used before agent registration.
Agent JWT (Authorization: Bearer <agent_jwt>) All capabilities with per-agent grant_status ("granted" / "not_granted" on every returned capability). Used after agent registration.

Query parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description
intent string No Natural language intent for server-side filtering (e.g. "send money")
cursor string No Opaque pagination cursor from a previous response's next_cursor
limit number No Maximum capabilities to return. Server MAY enforce a cap (e.g. 100). Default is server-defined.

Example response (http capabilities):

{
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "id": "check_balance",
      "title": "Check balance",
      "description": "Check account balance",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "GET",
        "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/accounts/{account_id}/balance",
        "input": {
          "parameters": [
            {
              "name": "account_id",
              "in": "path",
              "required": true,
              "schema": { "type": "string" }
            }
          ]
        }
      },
      "grant_status": "granted"
    },
    {
      "id": "transfer_domestic",
      "title": "Transfer funds domestically",
      "description": "Transfer funds domestically",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/transfers/domestic",
        "input": {
          "requestBody": {
            "required": true,
            "content": {
              "application/json": {
                "schema": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["amount", "currency", "destination_account"],
                  "properties": {
                    "amount": { "type": "number" },
                    "currency": { "type": "string" },
                    "destination_account": { "type": "string" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      },
      "grant_status": "granted"
    },
    {
      "id": "transfer_international",
      "title": "International wire transfer",
      "description": "International wire transfer",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/transfers/international"
      },
      "grant_status": "not_granted"
    }
  ]
}

Example response (custom invoke type):

{
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "id": "get_transactions",
      "title": "Get recent transactions",
      "description": "Get recent transactions",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "acme.stream",
        "channel": "deployments"
      },
      "grant_status": "granted"
    },
    {
      "id": "create_payment",
      "title": "Create payment",
      "description": "Create a new payment",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/payments"
      },
      "grant_status": "granted"
    }
  ]
}

Example response (mixed standard and custom types):

{
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "id": "list_issues",
      "title": "List issues",
      "description": "List GitHub issues",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "GET",
        "url": "https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "get_repo",
      "title": "Get repository",
      "description": "Get repository details",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "github.custom-tool",
        "tool_name": "get_repo"
      }
    }
  ]
}

Core fields (protocol-level, same for all types):

Field Type Required Description
id string Yes Stable machine-readable capability identifier. Used in registration, grants, and JWT restriction.
title string No Human-readable short label for the capability. Recommended for UIs and agent selection.
description string Yes What this capability does and when to use it.
invoke object Yes Invoke descriptor. Contains a required type field. All other fields are type-specific and optional at the protocol level. Invoke-specific input metadata belongs inside this object.
grant_status string No "granted" or "not_granted". Present on every returned capability when using agent JWT auth. Omitted otherwise.

The protocol does not define top-level invocation fields such as input_schema or output_schema at the capability core level. If an invoke type wants to describe inputs, outputs, parameters, or request bodies, that metadata SHOULD live inside invoke. Servers MAY include additional top-level fields as extensions, and clients SHOULD pass unrecognized capability fields through unchanged.

Pagination fields:

Field Type Description
next_cursor string? Opaque cursor to pass as ?cursor= for the next page. null if no more results.
has_more boolean true if more capabilities exist beyond this page.

Pagination is optional — servers with a small capability set MAY return all capabilities in a single response with has_more: false. Clients MUST handle both paginated and non-paginated responses.

5.2.1 Invoke Descriptors

The invoke object MUST contain a type field. All other fields are defined by the server and specific to that type. The protocol does not require any particular invoke type, but it defines a recommended standard profile for type = "http" in §5.2.2.

The agent reads the invoke descriptor and uses whatever tools, integrations, or reasoning it has available to fulfill the capability. Clients MAY provide helper tools or adapters for additional invoke types. Generic clients SHOULD support the standard HTTP profile so a server can expose a usable baseline without requiring a custom integration.

Examples of valid invoke descriptors:

HTTP endpoint:

{
  "type": "http",
  "method": "POST",
  "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/payments",
  "input": {
    "requestBody": {
      "required": true,
      "content": {
        "application/json": {
          "schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "required": ["amount", "currency"],
            "properties": {
              "amount": { "type": "number" },
              "currency": { "type": "string" }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

GraphQL:

{
  "type": "graphql",
  "endpoint": "https://api.example.com/graphql",
  "query": "query GetUser($id: ID!) { user(id: $id) { name email } }"
}

Documentation reference:

{
  "type": "docs",
  "url": "https://docs.example.com/api/send-email"
}

Custom / proprietary:

{
  "type": "acme.deploy",
  "cluster": "us-east-1",
  "runtime": "node20"
}

All of these are valid. The protocol treats invoke descriptors as type-tagged descriptors. Only type is universally required by the core protocol.

Clients SHOULD pass invoke descriptors through unchanged when listing capabilities to the agent. The agent decides how to use them.

5.2.2 Standard HTTP Invoke Profile

To improve interoperability across generic clients, this spec defines a recommended standard invoke profile for capabilities with invoke.type = "http".

Servers that want their capabilities to work out of the box across generic Agent Auth clients SHOULD expose at least one usable set of capabilities via this profile. Generic clients SHOULD provide a built-in HTTP invoke tool or equivalent adapter for this profile.

HTTP invoke fields:

Field Type Required Description
type string Yes Must be "http"
method string Yes HTTP method (for example "GET", "POST", "PATCH")
url string Yes Absolute URL to call
headers object No Additional headers to send. Client-generated headers (e.g., Authorization) MUST take precedence over values specified here.
interaction_mode string No Invoke hint: "sync", "stream", or "async"
input object No OpenAPI-inspired input description for parameters and request body.

The input object is an optional OpenAPI-compatible subset that lets a capability describe how invocation arguments map onto an HTTP request. This spec intentionally keeps the subset small so servers can generate it easily and clients can implement it consistently.

invoke.input fields:

Field Type Required Description
parameters array No OpenAPI-style parameter definitions. Supported in values are "path", "query", and "header".
requestBody object No OpenAPI-style request body definition for body-bearing methods.

Each entry in parameters follows this subset:

Field Type Required Description
name string Yes Parameter name
in string Yes "path", "query", or "header"
required boolean No Whether the parameter is required
schema object Yes JSON Schema describing the parameter value

If requestBody is present, clients SHOULD support:

Field Type Required Description
required boolean No Whether the request body is required
description string No Human-readable description of the request body
content object Yes Media-type map, following the OpenAPI content shape. Each key is a media type (e.g. "application/json"), and each value is an object containing a schema field.

Each entry in content follows this subset:

Field Type Required Description
schema object Yes JSON Schema describing the request body for this media type

For the initial profile, servers SHOULD use application/json as the media type key with a JSON Schema under content["application/json"].schema. Clients SHOULD use the media type key as the Content-Type header when sending the request.

Invocation rules:

  1. The client SHOULD send the current signed JWT as Authorization: Bearer <token> when invoking the capability.
  2. If invoke.input is present, the client SHOULD construct the request according to that schema:
    • parameters with in = "path" fill {name} placeholders in the URL
    • parameters with in = "query" become query string parameters
    • parameters with in = "header" become HTTP headers
    • requestBody becomes the HTTP request body. The client SHOULD use the first media type key in requestBody.content as the Content-Type header, following the OpenAPI convention.
  3. If invoke.input is not present and the url contains {name} placeholders, the client SHOULD substitute them from the invocation arguments by key and percent-encode the inserted values.
  4. If invoke.input is not present, remaining invocation arguments SHOULD be encoded as query parameters for GET, HEAD, and DELETE requests.
  5. If invoke.input is not present, remaining invocation arguments SHOULD be sent as the request body for POST, PUT, and PATCH with Content-Type: application/json.
  6. If interaction_mode is "sync" or omitted, the client waits for the HTTP response and returns it directly.
  7. If interaction_mode is "stream", the client SHOULD expose the response as a stream when the transport supports streaming.
  8. If interaction_mode is "async", the server returns HTTP 202 Accepted with a JSON body that MUST include a status_url field (absolute URL) for polling. The client SHOULD poll the status_url with GET requests until the operation completes. The polling response MUST include a status field with one of "pending", "completed", or "failed". When status is "completed", the response SHOULD include a result field containing the operation output. When status is "failed", the response SHOULD include an error field following the error format in §5.11. The server MAY include a Retry-After header or a retry_after field (in seconds) to suggest a polling interval.

This profile is a recommended interoperability baseline, not the only valid invoke model. Clients MAY support additional invoke types, and servers MAY publish custom types when they need behavior that does not fit the HTTP profile.

5.2.3 General Notes

Capability IDs are opaque strings. The protocol does not define a required naming format — servers MAY use any stable string. Clients and agents MUST treat capability IDs as uninterpreted identifiers.

Servers MAY include additional fields on capability objects beyond those defined above. Clients SHOULD pass through unrecognized fields without dropping them.

For generic clients that support multiple providers, the client MAY prefix capability IDs with the provider name when presenting them to the agent (e.g. list_issues → github.list_issues) to avoid naming collisions. This is a client-local convention only. The client MUST map prefixed capability IDs back to the original capability objects before execution. The server never sees prefixed client-local identifiers.

5.3 Agent Registration

POST /agent/register

Registers a new agent through a host.

Authorization: Host JWT with agent key claims (§4.2). The host JWT MUST include agent_public_key or agent_jwks_url for registration.

Request body:

{
  "name": "Bank balance checker",
  "capability_ids": ["check_balance", "transfer_domestic"],
  "mode": "delegated",
  "reason": "User asked to check account balances and make a domestic transfer",
}
Field Type Required Description
name string Yes Human-readable name for the agent. Displayed in dashboards, approval screens, and audit logs.
capability_ids string[] No Requested capability IDs. If omitted, the server grants the host's default capabilities.
mode string No "delegated" (default) or "autonomous"
reason string No Human-readable reason for the request. Displayed to the user on the approval screen.

Response (auto-approved):

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "host_id": "hst_xyz789",
  "name": "Bank Balance Checker",
  "mode": "delegated",
  "status": "active",
  "agent_capability_grants": [
    { "capability_id": "check_balance", "status": "active" },
    { "capability_id": "transfer_domestic", "status": "active" }
  ]
}

Response (approval required):

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "host_id": "hst_xyz789",
  "name": "Bank Balance Checker",
  "mode": "delegated",
  "status": "pending",
  "agent_capability_grants": [
    { "capability_id": "check_balance", "status": "pending" },
    { "capability_id": "transfer_domestic", "status": "pending" }
  ],
  "approval": {
    "method": "device_authorization",
    "verification_uri": "https://bank.com/device",
    "verification_uri_complete": "https://bank.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
    "user_code": "ABCD-1234",
    "expires_in": 300,
    "interval": 5
  }
}

The client polls GET /agent/status (§5.5) at the interval rate until the agent's status changes from "pending" to "active" (or the request expires).

5.4 Request Capability

POST /agent/request-capability

Requests additional capabilities for an existing agent.

Request headers:

Authorization: Bearer <agent_jwt>
Content-Type: application/json

Request body:

{
  "capability_ids": ["transfer_international"],
  "reason": "User requested an international wire transfer"
}
Field Type Required Description
capability_ids string[] Yes Additional capability IDs to request for this agent
reason string No Human-readable explanation for why the agent is requesting these capabilities. Servers SHOULD store this on the resulting grant records for auditability.

Response (auto-approved — within host defaults):

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "status": "granted",
  "agent_capability_grants": [
    { "capability_id": "transfer_international", "status": "active" }
  ]
}

Response (approval required):

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "status": "pending",
  "agent_capability_grants": [
    { "capability_id": "transfer_international", "status": "pending" }
  ],
  "approval": {
    "method": "device_authorization",
    "verification_uri": "https://bank.com/device",
    "verification_uri_complete": "https://bank.com/device?code=WXYZ-5678",
    "user_code": "WXYZ-5678",
    "expires_in": 300,
    "interval": 5
  }
}

The client polls GET /agent/status (§5.5) at the interval rate to track pending capability requests. When a capability grant's status changes from "pending" to "active", it was approved. When it changes to "denied", it was denied.

5.5 Status

GET /agent/status

Returns the current status of an agent and its granted capabilities.

Authorization: Host JWT. Requires ?agent_id=... query param. The server MUST verify the agent was created through this host.

Request headers:

Authorization: Bearer <host_jwt>
Content-Type: application/json
Field Type Required Description
agent_id string Yes The agent's ID

Response:

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "host_id": "hst_xyz789",
  "status": "active",
  "agent_capability_grants": [
    { "capability_id": "check_balance", "status": "active", "granted_by": "user_alice" },
    { "capability_id": "transfer_domestic", "status": "active", "granted_by": "user_alice" }
  ],
  "mode": "delegated",
  "user_id": "user_alice",
  "created_at": "2026-02-25T10:00:00Z",
  "last_used_at": "2026-02-25T14:30:00Z",
  "expires_at": "2026-02-26T10:00:00Z"
}

The agent_capability_grants array is the single source of truth for capability state. Each grant has a status field: "active" (granted and usable), "pending" (awaiting approval), or "denied" (user rejected).

5.6 Revoke

POST /agent/revoke

Revokes an agent permanently. The linked user MAY also revoke agents through the server's UI or API.

Authorization: Host JWT. The server MUST verify the agent was created through this host.

Request body:

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123"
}

Response:

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "status": "revoked"
}

5.7 Key Rotation

POST /agent/rotate-key

Replaces an agent's public key. The old key stops working immediately.

Authorization: Agent JWT. The request MUST be signed with the agent's current key.

Request body:

{
  "public_key": { "kty": "OKP", "crv": "Ed25519", "x": "new-key..." }
}

Response:

{
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "status": "active"
}

5.8 Rotate Host Key

POST /agent/host/rotate-key

Replaces a host's public key in-place. All agents under the host continue to work — only the host's authentication key changes. The old key stops working immediately.

Authorization: Host JWT signed with the current key.

Request headers:

Authorization: Bearer <host_jwt>
Content-Type: application/json

Request body:

{
  "public_key": { "kty": "OKP", "crv": "Ed25519", "x": "new-key..." }
}

Response:

{
  "host_id": "hst_xyz789",
  "status": "active"
}

The server replaces the stored public key. Future host JWTs must be signed with the new key. Existing agents, capability grants, user_id, and trust are unaffected.

This endpoint is for inline key hosts only. JWKS-based hosts rotate by updating the key at their JWKS URL — servers pick up the new key on their next fetch. See §9.7 for rotation security considerations.

5.9 Revoke Host

POST /agent/host/revoke

Revokes a host and all agents created through it.

Authorization: Host JWT — the host revokes itself. The server revokes the host and all agents under it. The linked user MAY also revoke hosts through the server's UI or API.

Request headers:

Authorization: Bearer <host_jwt>

Response:

{
  "host_id": "hst_abc123",
  "status": "revoked",
  "agents_revoked": 3
}

5.10 Introspect

POST /agent/introspect

Validates an agent JWT and returns the agent's current status and granted capabilities. Follows the model of RFC 7662 (OAuth 2.0 Token Introspection). This endpoint enables resource servers that are separate from the auth server to verify agent JWTs without direct access to agent public keys or the auth server's database.

Request headers:

Content-Type: application/json

Request body:

{
  "token": "eyJhbGciOiJFZERTQSIs..."
}
Field Type Required Description
token string Yes The agent JWT to validate

Response (active):

{
  "active": true,
  "agent_id": "agt_abc123",
  "host_id": "hst_xyz789",
  "user_id": "user_alice",
  "agent_capability_grants": [
    { "capability_id": "check_balance", "status": "active" },
    { "capability_id": "transfer_domestic", "status": "active" }
  ],
  "mode": "delegated",
  "expires_at": "2026-02-28T14:30:00Z"
}

Response (inactive):

{
  "active": false
}
Field Type Description
active boolean true if the JWT is valid, the agent is active, and the signature verified. false for any reason (expired JWT, revoked agent, bad signature, unknown agent).
agent_id string Agent ID (from JWT sub claim). Only present when active: true.
host_id string Host that created this agent. Only present when active: true.
user_id string? Linked user, if any. Only present when active: true.
agent_capability_grants array Per-capability grant status. If the JWT contained a capability_ids claim, only capabilities in that claim are included. Only present when active: true.
mode string "delegated" or "autonomous". Only present when active: true.
expires_at string? Agent session expiry (ISO 8601). Only present when active: true.

The auth server performs the full verification flow (§4.5) and returns an active/inactive response.

5.11 Error Format

All endpoints use a standard error response format:

{
  "error": "error_code",
  "message": "Human-readable description of what went wrong"
}

The error field is a machine-readable code (snake_case string). The message field is a human-readable explanation for debugging — clients SHOULD NOT parse it programmatically.

Common error codes (apply to all endpoints):

Status Error Code Meaning Client Action
400 invalid_request Malformed JSON, missing required fields, or invalid parameter types Fix request and retry
401 invalid_jwt JWT is invalid, expired, or signature failed Re-sign and retry
403 agent_revoked Agent has been revoked Cannot recover — create new agent
403 agent_expired Agent session has expired Re-register or reactivate
403 absolute_lifetime_exceeded Agent's absolute lifetime has elapsed Cannot recover — create new agent
403 agent_pending Agent is still pending approval Wait for approval to complete
403 host_revoked Host has been revoked Cannot recover
403 host_pending Host is still pending approval Wait for approval to complete
403 unauthorized Caller is not authorized for this operation (e.g. host revoking an agent it didn't create) Check authorization
429 rate_limited Too many requests Retry after delay
500 internal_error Server-side failure Retry or report

Per-endpoint error codes:

Endpoint Status Error Code Meaning
POST /agent/register 400 unsupported_mode Requested mode (delegated/autonomous) is not supported by this server
POST /agent/register 400 unsupported_algorithm Agent's key algorithm is not in the server's supported set
POST /agent/register 400 invalid_capabilities One or more requested capability IDs don't exist or are blocked
POST /agent/register 409 agent_exists An agent with this public key is already registered
POST /agent/request-capability 400 invalid_capabilities One or more requested capability IDs don't exist or are blocked
POST /agent/request-capability 409 already_granted All requested capabilities are already granted
Any agent-authenticated 403 capability_not_granted The agent does not have this capability
Any agent-authenticated 403 limit_exceeded The agent has this capability but the request exceeds its limits
POST /agent/revoke 404 agent_not_found Agent ID doesn't exist
POST /agent/host/revoke 404 host_not_found Host ID doesn't exist
POST /agent/rotate-key 400 unsupported_algorithm New key's algorithm is not supported
POST /agent/host/rotate-key 400 unsupported_algorithm New key's algorithm is not supported
GET /agent/status 404 agent_not_found Agent ID doesn't exist
POST /agent/introspect 404 agent_not_found Agent ID doesn't exist
POST /agent/introspect 404 host_not_found Host ID doesn't exist

Servers MAY return additional error codes beyond those listed. Clients SHOULD handle unknown error codes gracefully by falling back to the HTTP status code.

For CIBA endpoints (§8.2), the authorization and token polling flow follows RFC 8628 (OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant). Error responses for these endpoints use the error codes defined in RFC 8628 §3.5.

6. Client

The client sits between the agent and the server. It manages host and agent keys, communicates with servers, and exposes a standard set of tools that agents call to discover, register, authenticate, and execute capabilities. A client can be an MCP server, a CLI, an SDK, or any process that implements these tools.

The protocol does not define how agents communicate with clients. A client may run in the same process or machine as the agent (e.g. a local MCP server, an embedded SDK) — in this case the agent calls tools directly with no authentication required. Alternatively, a client may be a remote service that the agent reaches over a network, in which case the agent-to-client channel requires its own authentication (OAuth, API keys, session tokens, or whatever the transport provides). This is outside the scope of this protocol.

6.1 list_providers

Returns all providers the client knows about. This is typically the first tool an agent calls — it shows what services are available before the agent decides which one to use. Providers can come from previous discoveries, pre-configured lists, or user setup.

Single-provider clients (e.g. GitHub's own MCP server) MAY omit this tool since the agent already knows the provider.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
(none) Returns all known providers

Behavior:

Returns the list of providers with a name and description for each. The agent reads the list and picks the relevant provider for its task.

Response shape:

[
  { "name": "github", "description": "Code repositories, issues, and pull requests" },
  { "name": "gmail", "description": "Google Email — read, send, and manage emails" },
  { "name": "bank", "description": "Banking services — accounts, transfers, and payments" }
]

6.2 discover

Resolves a service into a usable provider configuration. This tool serves two distinct use cases:

  • Direct discovery — the agent already has a URL (e.g. the user said "connect to example.com"). The client fetches the service's discovery document from /.well-known/agent-configuration and stores it.
  • Intent-based search — the agent describes what it needs (e.g. "send emails") and the client queries a registry to find matching services.

An agent may call this as its first action, after list_providers returns nothing relevant, or at any point when a new service is needed. Clients that are pre-configured for specific services MAY omit this tool.

*Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
intent string No Natural language description of what the agent needs (e.g. "banking", "send emails")
url string No Direct service URL — SHOULD require user confirmation or allowlist check (see Security Considerations)

At least one of intent or url MUST be provided.

Behavior:

  1. If url is provided: fetch {url}/.well-known/agent-configuration directly. Clients SHOULD require user confirmation or check against an allowlist before fetching arbitrary URLs.
  2. If intent is provided: query a registry (§6.10) to find matching services. Return matching providers for the agent to choose from.
  3. Store the resolved provider configuration for use by subsequent tools.

6.3 list_capabilities

Returns available capabilities. All parameters are optional — a bare list_capabilities() returns everything the server offers.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
provider string No Provider name (from list_providers). Required for multi-provider clients to target a specific service. Single-provider clients omit this.
intent string No Natural language intent, passed to the server for server-side filtering (e.g. "send money"). The server returns only capabilities relevant to the intent.
agent_id string No If provided, response includes grant status per capability ("granted" / "not_granted").
cursor string No Pagination cursor from a previous response. When provided, returns the next page of results.

Behavior:

  1. Determine which server to call (from provider, or the client's default if single-provider)
  2. Call GET /agent/capabilities (§5.2) with the appropriate parameters. Authenticate with a host JWT, or an agent JWT if agent_id is provided.
  3. If the response has has_more: true, the client returns the first page along with the cursor. The agent decides whether to request more by calling list_capabilities again with the cursor.

The client SHOULD return capabilities unchanged, including invoke descriptors the client does not itself support — the agent decides how to use them.

Response shape:

{
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "id": "transfer_domestic",
      "title": "Transfer funds domestically",
      "description": "Transfer funds domestically",
      "grant_status": "not_granted",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/transfers/domestic"
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "check_balance",
      "title": "Check balance",
      "description": "Check account balance",
      "grant_status": "granted",
      "invoke": {
        "type": "http",
        "method": "GET",
        "url": "https://api.bank.com/v1/accounts/balance"
      }
    }
  ]
}

The grant_status field is present when the request is authenticated with an agent JWT. It indicates whether the agent has already been granted this capability.

6.4 connect_agent

Connects an agent to a service. This generates a keypair, registers the agent on the server, handles any required approval, and stores the connection locally. The agent gets its own identity, keypair, and set of granted capabilities.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
provider string Yes Provider name (from list_providers) or service URL
capability_ids string[] No Capability IDs to request. If omitted, the server decides which capabilities to grant based on its own policy.
mode string No "delegated" (default) or "autonomous"

Behavior:

  1. Look up the provider's configuration (trigger discover if not cached)
  2. Generate an agent keypair
  3. Sign a host JWT containing the agent's public key (see §4.2 for JWT construction)
  4. Call POST /agent/register (§5.3) with the host JWT, capability IDs, and mode
  5. If status: "active" → store the agent connection and return agent_id + granted capabilities
  6. If status: "pending" → initiate the approval flow returned by the server (§8). Poll for status until the agent is approved, rejected, or the approval expires. On approval, store the connection and return agent_id + granted capabilities. On rejection or timeout, report the outcome to the agent.

6.5 sign_jwt

Returns a signed agent JWT for authenticating capability invocations. The agent does not hold its own private key — the client does (§4.1). This tool lets the client remain the gatekeeper while giving the agent a short-lived token it can use with whatever tools it has (HTTP clients, browser tools, code execution, etc.).

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
agent_id string Yes The agent's ID
capability_ids string[] No Restrict this JWT to specific capabilities. If omitted, the JWT is valid for all the agent's granted capabilities.

Behavior:

  1. If capability_ids is provided, verify all requested capabilities are within the agent's granted set — reject if any are not
  2. Sign an agent JWT (see §4.3 for claims and construction). The JWT SHOULD expire within 60 seconds.
  3. Return the signed JWT string

The client MAY refuse to sign (e.g. agent is revoked, rate limit exceeded). The client MAY restrict the JWT to specific capabilities even if the agent didn't request it. See §4.4 for optional proof-of-possession profiles.

Response shape:

{
  "token": "eyJhbGciOiJFZERTQSIs...",
  "expires_at": 1730000060
}

6.6 request_capability

Requests additional capabilities for an already-connected agent. This is how an agent escalates beyond its initial grant — for example, an agent that was granted read access now needs write access.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
agent_id string Yes The agent's ID
capability_ids string[] Yes Additional capability IDs to request
reason string No Why the agent needs these capabilities

Behavior:

  1. Sign an agent JWT and call POST /agent/request-capability (§5.4)
  2. If status: "granted" → return the newly granted capabilities
  3. If status: "pending" → initiate the approval flow returned by the server (§8). Poll for status until approved or denied.
  4. Return the outcome to the agent

Response shape:

{
  "granted": ["write_document", "delete_document"],
  "pending": [],
  "denied": []
}

6.7 disconnect_agent

Revokes the agent on the server and removes the stored connection locally. This is permanent — the agent cannot be reactivated after disconnecting.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
agent_id string Yes The agent's ID

Behavior:

  1. Look up the host identity associated with this agent connection
  2. Sign a host JWT and call POST /agent/revoke (§5.6)
  3. Remove the stored connection data (keypair, config)
  4. Return confirmation

6.8 agent_status

Checks the health and status of an existing agent connection.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
agent_id string Yes The agent's ID

Behavior:

  1. Look up the host identity associated with this agent connection
  2. Sign a host JWT and call GET /agent/status with ?agent_id=...
  3. Return agent status, granted capabilities, and connection health

6.9 http_request

Optional. Executes a capability with invoke.type = "http" on behalf of an agent. The agent provides a capability ID and arguments; the client looks up the invoke descriptor, constructs the request per the standard HTTP invoke profile (§5.2.2), signs the JWT, and makes the call.

This tool exists so agents don't need their own HTTP tools to invoke the most common invoke type. Clients MAY omit it — agents with their own HTTP capabilities can use sign_jwt (§6.5) and make requests directly.

For capabilities with non-HTTP invoke types, the client MAY provide additional invoke tools beyond what this spec defines. A proprietary client, for example, might offer tools for its own custom invoke types.

Input:

Parameter Type Required Description
agent_id string Yes The agent's ID
capability_id string Yes The capability to invoke (must have invoke.type = "http")
arguments object No Invocation arguments, mapped onto the request per the capability's invoke.input descriptor

Behavior:

  1. Look up the capability's invoke descriptor from the cached capabilities. Reject if invoke.type is not "http".
  2. Construct the HTTP request per §5.2.2 invocation rules (substitute path parameters, build query string, set headers, format body)
  3. Sign an agent JWT scoped to this capability and set Authorization: Bearer <agent_jwt>
  4. Make the request and return the response

6.10 Registry

A registry is a searchable index of services that support Agent Auth. It is a client-side discovery aid, not part of the core server protocol — the registry URL is configured on the client.

When an agent calls discover with an intent, the client queries its configured registry to find matching providers. Clients SHOULD prefer registry lookups over fetching arbitrary URLs provided by agents.

Registries MAY be public (open to all services), private (enterprise-only), or bundled with the client as a static list.

Search interface:

To be compatible with generic clients, a registry MUST implement the following endpoint:

GET {registry_url}/api/search?intent={intent}&limit={limit}

Parameter Type Required Description
intent string Yes Natural-language description of what the agent needs (e.g. "send emails", "deploy websites")
limit number No Maximum number of results (default: 10)

Response shape:

{
  "providers": [
    {
      "protocol_version": "1.0",
      "provider_name": "example",
      "description": "Example service",
      "issuer": "https://example.com",
      "algorithms": ["EdDSA"],
      "modes": ["delegated", "autonomous"],
      "approval_methods": ["device_authorization"],
      "endpoints": { ... },
      "jwks_uri": "https://example.com/.well-known/jwks.json"
    }
  ],
  "intent": "the original intent",
  "count": 1
}

Each entry in providers is a standard discovery document (§5.1). The client stores matching providers the same way it stores directly discovered ones.

Service registration:

How services are added to a registry is implementation-specific. A registry MAY accept submissions via API, require manual review, auto-discover from a crawl, or any other mechanism. The only requirement for client compatibility is the search interface above.

7. Data Model

This section defines the logical fields the protocol operates on. These are not a mandated database schema — implementations MAY store these fields however they choose, as long as the protocol-facing behavior is preserved.

7.1 Host

Field Type Description
id string Unique identifier
name string? Human-readable name identifying the environment (e.g. MacBook-Pro", "ChatGPT"). Informational only — servers MUST NOT use it for authorization.
public_key JWK? Ed25519 public key (see §4.1). Present when inline key registration is used.
jwks_url string? JWKS endpoint URL. Present when JWKS-based registration is used.
user_id string? ID of the owning user in the server's auth system. Set when a user approves and trusts the host. Drives authorization (revocation, management).
default_capability_ids string[] Default capabilities for agents created through this host
status enum active, pending, revoked, rejected
activated_at datetime? Last activation
expires_at datetime? TTL deadline
last_used_at datetime? Last host JWT usage
created_at datetime Created
updated_at datetime Last modified

Clients SHOULD auto-detect the name by combining the tool/IDE name with the device hostname (e.g. "MacBook-Pro"). When no tool is detected, the device hostname alone is sufficient.

7.2 Agent

Field Type Description
id string Unique identifier
name string Human-readable name for the agent
host_id string (FK) The host that created this agent (required)
user_id string? (FK) The user this agent acts on behalf of. Set from the host's user_id or session auth.
public_key JWK? Ed25519 public key (see §4.1). Present when inline key registration is used.
jwks_url string? JWKS endpoint URL. Present when JWKS-based registration is used.
status enum active, pending, expired, revoked, rejected, claimed
mode string "delegated" or "autonomous". Immutable after creation (§2.2).
last_used_at datetime? Last authenticated request
activated_at datetime? Last activation
expires_at datetime? TTL deadline
created_at datetime Created
updated_at datetime Last modified

7.3 Agent Capability Grant

Agent capabilities are stored as individual agent capability grant records rather than flat arrays on the agent. This enables per-capability metadata (expiry, grant source, reason) and granular revocation.

Field Type Description
id string Unique identifier
agent_id string (FK) The agent this capability grant belongs to
capability_id string The granted capability identifier
status enum active or pending
granted_by string? (FK) User who granted this permission
reason string? Why this permission was granted or requested
expires_at datetime? When this specific permission expires (independent of agent TTL)
created_at datetime Created
updated_at datetime Last modified

An agent's effective capabilities are all agent capability grants where status = "active" and expires_at is null or in the future. Pending capabilities are grants where status = "pending".

Implementations MAY use flat arrays on the agent model instead of a separate table, but MUST support at minimum per-capability status tracking (active vs pending).

7.3.1 Multi-Grantor Authorization

Different users can grant different capabilities to the same agent. Each agent capability grant row has its own granted_by, tracking who authorized it. Grants are independently revocable — revoking one user's grant does not affect another's.

Example: A single agent with three grants from three different users:

capability_id granted_by Use case
read_emails user_alice Alice granted access to her emails
create_issue user_bob Bob granted access to his repository
audit_log admin Admin granted global audit access

Revoking Alice's read_emails grant does not affect Bob's create_issue or the admin's audit_log.

8. Approval Methods

When a registration or capability request requires user consent, the server returns an approval object describing how the client should facilitate it. This section defines the approval methods the protocol supports.

Servers MUST support device authorization (§8.1) as the baseline approval method. All other methods are OPTIONAL.

8.1 Device Authorization (RFC 8628)

The baseline approval method. Every conformant server MUST support this. Used when the client cannot directly interact with the user — the user navigates to a URL and enters a code on a separate device. Polling behavior, error responses, and security considerations follow RFC 8628.

  1. Server returns verification_uri + user_code + expires_in + interval in the approval object
  2. Client presents the URL and user code to the user (opens browser, displays code)
  3. User authenticates and approves at the URL
  4. Client polls GET /agent/status (§5.5) at the interval rate until the agent's status changes from "pending" to "active" (approved), "rejected" (denied), or the request expires

Agent Auth-specific behavior:

  • Agent ID is reusable after timeout only. A timed-out pending agent stays in pending state. The client MAY retry by calling POST /agent/register with the same agent keypair — the server SHOULD recognize the existing agent and issue a new approval flow rather than creating a duplicate.
  • User denial is terminal for that attempt. If the approval flow returns access_denied, the server SHOULD transition the agent to rejected (not pending). The client MUST NOT automatically retry a denied registration.
  • Pending agent cleanup. Servers SHOULD periodically clean up agents that remain in pending state beyond a server-defined threshold (e.g. 24 hours, 7 days). Cleaned-up pending agents are deleted, not revoked — they never became active.

8.2 CIBA (Client Initiated Backchannel Authentication)

Used when the server can reach the user directly (e.g. mobile app, email, in-app notification). Servers SHOULD implement CIBA — it provides a better experience than device authorization when the server already knows the user's identity, since the user doesn't need to navigate to a URL.

  1. Client calls POST /agent/ciba/authorize with login_hint (email, phone, user ID) and optional capability_ids, binding_message
  2. Server creates a pending CIBA request and returns expires_in, interval
  3. Server notifies the user through its notification channel (push notification, email, SMS, in-app)
  4. User approves or denies on their device via POST /agent/ciba/approve or POST /agent/ciba/deny
  5. Client polls GET /agent/status (§5.5) at the interval rate until the agent's status changes

CIBA endpoints:

Endpoint Method Description
/agent/ciba/authorize POST Initiate a CIBA authentication request
/agent/ciba/approve POST Approve a pending CIBA request (user-facing)
/agent/ciba/deny POST Deny a pending CIBA request (user-facing)
/agent/ciba/pending GET List pending CIBA requests for the current user

Approval result is observed through GET /agent/status (§5.5), not a separate token endpoint. The client polls the status endpoint at the interval rate.

Servers implementing CIBA MUST follow the security considerations in CIBA Core §13 — particularly around rate limiting, user enumeration, and notification fatigue.

8.3 Real-Time Notification (Optional)

Polling is the baseline for all approval methods. However, servers MAY offer real-time notification as an alternative to reduce unnecessary polling.

If the server supports push notification, the approval object in the response MAY include a notification_url field:

{
  "approval": {
    "method": "device_authorization",
    "verification_uri": "https://bank.com/device",
    "user_code": "ABCD-1234",
    "expires_in": 300,
    "interval": 5,
    "notification_url": "https://auth.bank.com/agent/events/agt_abc123"
  }
}

The client connects to notification_url via SSE and receives an event when the approval completes, is denied, or expires. If the connection fails or notification_url is absent, the client MUST fall back to polling GET /agent/status.

8.4 Server-Defined

The server MAY implement custom approval flows. The approval object in responses carries whatever information the client needs to facilitate approval.

8.5 Method Selection

When a server supports multiple approval methods, it decides which one to use based on context. Clients MAY indicate a preferred method, but the server makes the final decision and returns the chosen method in the approval object.

General guidance:

  • CIBA when the server already knows the user (e.g. host has a user_id or client provides a login_hint)
  • Device authorization when the user is unknown and must navigate to a URL themselves

9. Security Considerations

9.1 Private Keys

Private keys MUST never be transmitted to or stored on the server. The server stores only public keys or JWKS URLs.

9.2 JWT Lifetime

JWTs SHOULD expire within 60 seconds. Short lifetimes limit misuse even without proof of possession.

9.3 Proof of Possession

The base protocol does not require proof of possession. Short-lived JWTs over TLS are sufficient for many applications.

For sensitive operations, servers SHOULD support a standard proof-of-possession profile:

  • DPoP — the client sends a separate DPoP proof JWT on each request, and the agent JWT SHOULD include cnf.jkt
  • mTLS — the client authenticates with a TLS client certificate, and the agent JWT SHOULD include cnf.x5t#S256

This keeps the baseline simple for average apps while giving higher-assurance deployments a standards-aligned path.

9.4 Replay Protection

Servers MUST reject duplicate jti values within the JWT's lifetime window.

9.5 Revocation Cascade

Revoking a host MUST cascade to all agents under it.

9.6 Capability Validation

Servers SHOULD validate requested capability IDs against a known set. Servers MAY maintain a blocked capability list.

9.7 Host Key Rotation

Inline key hosts rotate via POST /agent/host/rotate-key (§5.8). Since the client does not maintain a durable registry of every server a host is registered on, inline rotation is limited to servers the client currently knows about. For key compromise, revoke the host entirely — a compromised key means you cannot trust the rotation request itself.

JWKS-based hosts rotate by updating their endpoint — the server picks up new keys on the next cache refresh (recommended: every 5 minutes or on kid miss). The old key SHOULD remain in the JWKS for a grace period to avoid rejecting in-flight requests.

Hosts that register across many servers SHOULD prefer JWKS-based registration, since key rotation propagates automatically without the client needing to contact each server individually.

9.8 Agent Key Compromise

If an agent's private key is leaked, anyone holding it can impersonate that agent. This is inherent to any keypair-based system (same as a leaked SSH key or OAuth token). However, unlike bearer tokens, the agent's private key never traverses the wire — it can only leak from the client's local storage.

Mitigations:

  • Key rotation. Use POST /agent/rotate-key to immediately invalidate the compromised key and replace it with a new one.
  • Short TTL. Agents with short session_ttl values auto-expire, limiting the window of compromise.
  • Revocation. If compromise is detected, revoke the agent immediately.
  • Secure storage. Clients SHOULD store agent private keys securely (keychain, encrypted file, memory-only).

After registration, the agent authenticates independently with its own keypair — the host is not involved in every request. If binding agent requests to the host is required, implementations can enforce this at the server level.

9.9 Discovery Security

Discovery of arbitrary URLs is a prompt injection vector. A malicious prompt can trick an agent into calling discover(url: "https://evil.com"), which returns a fake configuration. The client then sends the host JWT to the attacker's "register" endpoint.

Mitigations:

  • Registry-first discovery. Clients SHOULD resolve new services through a trusted registry (§6.10) using intent-based search rather than arbitrary URLs.
  • User confirmation. If discover is called with a direct URL, the client SHOULD require user confirmation before fetching the configuration.
  • Allowlists. Clients MAY maintain an allowlist of trusted service URLs.
  • Proof on host-authenticated registration. For discovered servers (not pre-configured), clients SHOULD use DPoP or an equivalent request-specific proof on host-authenticated registration requests so they cannot be replayed to a different endpoint.
  • TLS verification. Clients MUST verify TLS certificates when fetching discovery endpoints.

9.10 Approval Gravity

User approval is the primary trust boundary in this protocol — for unknown host registration, capability escalation, and account linking.

First-time host approval is the highest-risk moment: the server knows nothing about the host beyond a self-signed keypair. Servers SHOULD present the requested capabilities clearly and warn if the host is requesting unusually broad access.

Capability escalation deserves the same scrutiny. Servers SHOULD NOT auto-approve high-risk capabilities even for linked hosts. Servers MUST calibrate approval requirements to the sensitivity of their capabilities — the protocol provides the mechanism (approval flows, fresh auth, 2FA), but the server decides which to enforce.

9.11 Approval Self-Authorization

Agents often run with access to the user's browser or session (via browser tools, MCP, automation frameworks). This creates a unique threat: an agent could navigate to the device authorization verification_uri and approve its own registration without the user being physically present.

This is not a concern in traditional OAuth because the client requesting authorization doesn't control the user's browser. In Agent Auth, it often does.
